step.title: Step 13
step.actionDisplay: DOWN -> RIGHT -> RIGHT -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 7 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 33, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Tiles (O+b block) at rows 15-19, cols 34-38, blocked from UP by B interior - Target b pattern at rows 11-13, cols 35-37 (inside B interior) - Each action moves tiles 5 rows/cols - At cols 39-43, tiles can move UP freely (not blocked by B interior) - At rows 20-24, tiles can move RIGHT freely (no G walls)
- Plan: DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT (7 actions to get b tiles to rows 12-14, cols 34-38, overlapping target at rows 12-13)
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 32 actions; a strong score needs about 22 or fewer. You are 1.5x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Let me analyze the transition history carefully:
Key observations:
1. The O and b blocks move together as a unit
2. Each move shifts them by 5 rows/cols
3. When they're at rows 15-19, cols 34-38, UP doesn't work (blocked)
4. When they're at rows 15-19, cols 39-43, UP works fine
5. When they're at rows 20-24, RIGHT doesn't work (blocked by G walls)
6. The blocks are currently at rows 15-19, cols 34-38
The target b pattern is at rows 11-13, cols 35-37. To reach it, the b block needs to be at rows 12-14, cols 34-38 (or similar).
The problem is that at cols 34-38, the blocks are blocked from moving UP beyond rows 15-19. But at cols 39-43, they CAN move UP freely.
So the strategy should be:
1. Move RIGHT to get to cols 39-43 (where UP is unblocked)
2. Move UP to get to the right row level
3. Move LEFT to get back to cols 34-38
But wait - RIGHT doesn't work when at rows 20-24 (blocked by G walls). RIGHT only works when at rows 15-19.
Current position: rows 15-19, cols 34-38
- RIGHT works here (no G walls blocking)
- UP is blocked here (B interior)
So I should:
1. RIGHT -> cols 39-43 (still rows 15-19)
2. UP -> rows 10-14 (cols 39-43)
3. LEFT -> cols 34-38 (rows 10-14)
This would place the b block at rows 12-14, cols 34-38, which overlaps with the target at rows 11-13, cols 35-37.
Let me verify: the b block is 3 rows tall (rows 17-19 currently). If I move UP from rows 15-19, it goes to rows 10-14. The b portion would be rows 12-14. The target is at rows 11-13. There's overlap at rows 12-13.
But wait, I need to check if RIGHT actually works at the current position. Looking at the transitions:
- Step 1: RIGHT worked when at rows 40-41, cols 34-38
- Step 21: RIGHT didn't work when at rows 20-24, cols 34-38
So RIGHT works at rows 15-19 but not at rows 20-24. Let me check... actually step 21 says RIGHT didn't move anything when at rows 20-24. But step 1 shows RIGHT worked at rows 40-41.
Hmm, the blocks are currently at rows 15-19. Let me check if RIGHT works there. Looking at the history, I don't see a RIGHT action at rows 15-19. Let me try RIGHT first.
Actually wait - looking more carefully at the transitions, when the blocks are at rows 15-19, cols 34-38, UP is blocked. But RIGHT should work (there's no G wall at that row range).
Let me try: RIGHT, UP, LEFT

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current position of O and b blocks
nodes = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] in ('O', 'b'):
        print(f"{n['color']}: boundary={n['boundary']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check current position
# O is at rows 15-16, cols 34-38
# b is at rows 17-19, cols 34-38
# Combined: rows 15-19, cols 34-38

# From transition history:
# - RIGHT works at rows 15-19 (no G walls)
# - UP is blocked at cols 34-38 (B interior)
# - UP works at cols 39-43 (no B interior)
# - RIGHT is blocked at rows 20-24 (G walls)

# Plan: RIGHT -> UP -> LEFT
# This moves blocks to cols 39-43, then UP to rows 10-14, then LEFT to cols 34-38
# Final position: rows 10-14, cols 34-38
# b block would be at rows 12-14, overlapping target at rows 11-13

print("\nExecuting: RIGHT, UP, LEFT")
action(['RIGHT', 'UP', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
